title.zh: 卷七十七 蓋諸葛劉鄭孫毌將何傳
title.en: Volume 77: Gai, Zhuge, Liu, Zheng, Sun, Wujiang and He
title.raw: 卷七十七 蓋諸葛劉鄭孫毌將何傳 Volume 77: Gai, Zhuge, Liu, Zheng, Sun, Wujiang and He

sentences.zh: 卷七十七 蓋諸葛劉鄭孫毋將何傳第四十七

translations.literal: Volume 77: "Biographies of Gai, Zhuge, Liu, Zheng, Sun, Wujiang, and He," number 47.
translations.idiomatic: Volume 77: Biographies of Gai, Zhuge, Liu, Zheng, Sun, Wujiang, and He—the forty-seventh series.

sentences.zh: 蓋寬饒

translations.literal: Gai Kuangrao.
translations.idiomatic: Gai Kuangrao.

sentences.zh: 蓋寬饒字次公，魏郡人也。

translations.literal: Gai Kuangrao, courtesy name Cigong, was a man of Wei Commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Gai Kuangrao, styled Cigong, came from Wei Commandery.

sentences.zh: 明經為郡文學，以孝廉為郎。

translations.literal: He passed the classics examination and became the commandery erudite-clerk; on a filial and incorrupt nomination he was appointed gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: He earned his classics degree as the commandery literary officer, then entered the court as a gentleman on the filial-incorrupt roster.

sentences.zh: 舉方正，對策高第，遷諫大夫，行郎中戶將事。

translations.literal: Recommended as upright and square, he scored high on the policy examination, was promoted to grandee remonstrant, and handled the duties of colonel of the household guard for the householders' gate.
translations.idiomatic: An "upright conduct" nomination and a top policy paper won him the remonstrant's post and acting charge of the household colonelcy at the inner gate.

sentences.zh: 劾奏衛將軍張安世子侍中陽都侯彭祖不下殿門，並連及安世居位無補。

translations.literal: He impeached the heir of General of the Guards Zhang Anshi—Pengzu, gentleman attendant at court and marquis of Yangdu—for failing to dismount at the palace hall gate, and linked the charge to Anshi's occupying office without real benefit.
translations.idiomatic: He denounced Zhang Pengzu, the marquis of Yangdu and son of Zhang Anshi, for riding through the hall gate, and argued that Zhang Anshi himself had grown useless in high place.

sentences.zh: 彭祖時實下門，寬饒坐舉奏大臣非是，左遷為衛司馬。

translations.literal: Pengzu had in fact dismounted; Kuangrao was convicted of impeaching a great minister wrongly and was demoted to colonel of the guards.
translations.idiomatic: Pengzu had actually obeyed the rule; the memorial backfired and Kuangrao was busted down to colonel of the palace guards.

sentences.zh: 先是時，衛司馬在部，見衛尉拜謁，常為衛官繇使市買。

translations.literal: Earlier, while the colonel of the guards was in his command, he would bow to the commandant of the guards and was regularly detailed by guard officers to run market errands.
translations.idiomatic: Before Kuangrao, the guard colonel had to kowtow to the commandant of guards and was treated like a messenger boy for shopping runs.

sentences.zh: 寬饒視事，案舊令，遂揖官屬以下行衛者。

translations.literal: When Kuangrao took office he consulted the old regulations and thereafter only bowed with hand clasp to subordinate officers who performed escort duty.
translations.idiomatic: On day one he reopened the statute book and replaced the kowtow with a ranked salute for men under him on escort duty.

sentences.zh: 衛尉私使寬饒出，寬饒以令詣官府門上謁辭。

translations.literal: The commandant of the guards privately ordered Kuangrao out on an errand; Kuangrao cited the rules and filed a formal refusal at the government gate.
translations.idiomatic: When the commandant tried to send him on a private chore, he walked to the yamen gate and entered a written refusal on the books.

sentences.zh: 尚書責問衛尉，由是衛官不復私使候、司馬。

translations.literal: The masters of writing rebuked the commandant of the guards; thereafter guard officers no longer privately detailed the colonel or marshal.
translations.idiomatic: The imperial secretariat called the commandant on the carpet, and the habit of drafting the colonel for private errands stopped cold.

sentences.zh: 候、司馬不拜，出先置衛，輒上奏辭，自此正焉。

translations.literal: The colonel and marshal did not perform the full bow; before going out to set the watch they always memorialized their leave—thus the practice was corrected.
translations.idiomatic: Colonels and marshals stopped the deep bow, filed written leave before each shift, and the ritual line was drawn for good.

sentences.zh: 寬饒初拜為司馬，未出殿門，斷其禪衣，令短離地，冠大冠，帶長劍，躬案行士卒廬室，視其飲食居處，有疾病者身自撫循臨問，加致醫藥，遇之甚有恩。

translations.literal: When Kuangrao was first appointed colonel, before he had left the palace hall he cut his knee-length under-robe short so it cleared the ground, donned the great cap, girded a long sword, personally inspected the barracks and huts, examined food and quarters, and for the sick personally comforted them, supplied physicians and drugs, and treated the men with great kindness.
translations.idiomatic: Fresh to the colonelcy he shortened his robe, belted on a long sword, toured every barrack, tasted the gruel, nursed the sick himself, and paid doctors out of his own pocket.

sentences.zh: 及歲盡交代，上臨饗罷衛卒，衛卒數千人皆叩頭自請，願復留共更一年，以報寬饒厚德。

translations.literal: At year's end when duties were handed over, the emperor came in person to feast the discharged guards; several thousand guards all kowtowed and begged to stay another year on rotation together to repay Kuangrao's deep kindness.
translations.idiomatic: When the year-end muster came and the emperor feasted the retiring guards, thousands begged on their knees to serve another tour under Kuangrao.

sentences.zh: 宣帝嘉之，以寬饒為太中大夫，使行風俗，多所稱舉貶黜，奉使稱意。

translations.literal: Emperor Xuan praised him, appointed Kuangrao grandee of the palace, sent him to tour customs and morals, and he commended or dismissed many officials; the mission pleased the throne.
translations.idiomatic: Xuan made him a palace grandee and sent him circuit-riding as moral inspector; his promotions and sackings matched what the court wanted to hear.

sentences.zh: 擢為司隸校尉，刺舉無所迴避，小大輒舉，所劾奏眾多，廷尉處其法，半用半不用，公卿貴戚及郡國吏繇使至長安，皆恐懼莫敢犯禁，京師為清。

translations.literal: He was promoted to metropolitan governor; in denouncing wrongdoing he never swerved, impeaching great and small alike; his memorials were so numerous that the commandant of justice applied the law to only half of them, yet high ministers, imperial in-laws, and clerks from commanderies and kingdoms on duty in Chang'an all feared him and dared not break the law—the capital grew clean.
translations.idiomatic: As metropolitan governor he subpoenaed everyone who mattered; the commandant of justice could only process half his docket, yet the capital went quiet because magnates stopped testing him.

sentences.zh: 平恩侯許伯入第，丞相、御史、將軍、中二千石皆賀，寬饒不行。

translations.literal: When Marquis of Ping'en Xu Bo entered his new mansion, the chancellor, imperial counsellor, generals, and ministers at two thousand piculs all attended the celebration; Kuangrao did not go.
translations.idiomatic: The whole peerage turned out for Xu Bo's housewarming; Kuangrao stayed home.

sentences.zh: 許伯請之，乃往，從西階上，東鄉特坐。

translations.literal: Xu Bo invited him, whereupon he went, ascended by the western stair, and took a seat facing east by himself.
translations.idiomatic: Xu had to coax him in; he climbed the west steps and sat alone on the host's east—the seat reserved for a peer.

sentences.zh: 許伯自酌曰：「蓋君後至。」

translations.literal: Xu Bo poured wine himself and said, "Lord Gai arrives late."
translations.idiomatic: Xu poured the wine himself and said, "Our late guest is Lord Gai."

sentences.zh: 寬饒曰：「無多酌我，我乃酒狂。」

translations.literal: Kuangrao said, "Do not fill my cup too full—I am a drunkard when wine flows."
translations.idiomatic: Kuangrao said, "Pour sparingly—I turn wild after a few cups."

sentences.zh: 丞相魏侯笑曰：「次公醒而狂，何必酒也？」

translations.literal: Chancellor Marquis of Wei laughed and said, "Cigong is mad while sober—what need of wine?"
translations.idiomatic: The Wei marquis laughed and said, "Cigong is born tipsy—wine only advertises it."

sentences.zh: 坐者畢屬目卑下之。

translations.literal: All who sat there fixed their eyes on him and thought him beneath them.
translations.idiomatic: The room stared and marked him as rude.

sentences.zh: 酒酣樂作，長信少府檀長卿起舞，為沐猴與狗鬥，坐皆大笑。

translations.literal: When the wine was hot and music began, the chamberlain for the palace of Great Faith, Tan Changqing, rose to dance a skit of a bath monkey fighting a dog; the company roared with laughter.
translations.idiomatic: Tan Changqing of the empress's palace danced a monkey-and-dog farce; the hall howled with laughter.

sentences.zh: 寬饒不說，卬視屋而歎曰：「美哉！

translations.literal: Kuangrao was displeased; he lifted his eyes to the rafters and sighed, "How splendid!"
translations.idiomatic: Kuangrao did not smile; he looked at the beams and said, "Fine sight!"

sentences.zh: 然富貴無常，忽則易人，此如傳捨，所閱多矣。

translations.literal: Yet wealth and honor are not constant; in a moment they change owners—this is like a relay inn, and I have watched such shifts many times.
translations.idiomatic: Yet rank and riches change hands like relay stations; he had watched the turnover too often to be charmed.

sentences.zh: 唯謹慎為得久，君侯可不戒哉！」

translations.literal: Only caution wins endurance—my lords, can you not take warning!"
translations.idiomatic: Only caution keeps a man long in his seat—my lords, will you never learn?"

sentences.zh: 因起趨出，劾奏長信少府以列卿而沐猴舞，失禮不敬。

translations.literal: Thereupon he rose and hurried out, and impeached the chamberlain for the palace of Great Faith for performing a monkey dance though he held rank among the nine ministers—disorderly and irreverent.
translations.idiomatic: He walked out and filed charges against Tan for doing a monkey act while holding ministerial rank—a breach of ritual.

sentences.zh: 上欲罪少府，許伯為謝，良久，上乃解。

translations.literal: The emperor wished to punish the chamberlain; Xu Bo apologized, and after a long while the emperor relented.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor meant to punish Tan until Xu Bo smoothed it over.

sentences.zh: 寬饒為人剛直高節，志在奉公。

translations.literal: Kuangrao was by nature rigidly upright and of lofty integrity; his ambition was to serve the public good.
translations.idiomatic: He was stiff-necked, high-principled, and lived for the job.

sentences.zh: 家貧。

translations.literal: His family was poor.
translations.idiomatic: His household was chronically broke.

sentences.zh: 奉錢月數千，半以給吏民為耳目言事者。

translations.literal: His salary was only a few thousand cash a month, half of which he gave to clerks and commoners who served as his eyes and ears for reporting affairs.
translations.idiomatic: He drew a clerk's pay and spent half of it buying informers in the streets.

sentences.zh: 身為司隸，子常步行自戍北邊，公廉如此。

translations.literal: Though he was metropolitan governor, his son commonly walked north to serve on the frontier garrisons—such was his public integrity.
translations.idiomatic: His son marched to the northern line on foot while he held the capital's highest police post—that was the man's standard.

sentences.zh: 然深刻喜陷害人，在位及貴戚人與為怨，又好言事刺譏，奸犯上意。

translations.literal: Yet he was harsh and deep, delighted in trapping people; those in office and powerful in-laws nursed grudges against him, and he loved to discuss affairs with cutting satire, often offending the emperor's mind.
translations.idiomatic: Yet he savored prosecutions, made enemies of every magnate, and lectured the throne until it smarted.

sentences.zh: 上以其儒者，優容之，然亦不得遷。

translations.literal: Because he was a scholar, the emperor treated him with indulgence, yet he was never promoted further.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor wrote him off as a harmless pedant and never moved him up.

sentences.zh: 同列後進或至九卿，寬饒自以行清能高，有益於國，而為凡庸所越，愈失意不快，數上疏諫爭。

translations.literal: Colleagues who had entered service later sometimes reached the Nine Ministers; Kuangrao thought his conduct clean and his ability high, beneficial to the state, yet vulgar mediocrities passed him by; he grew ever more frustrated and unhappy and repeatedly submitted memorials of remonstrance.
translations.idiomatic: Juniors leapt past him to the Nine Ministers while he stewed, convinced his integrity should have won him more.

sentences.zh: 太子庶子王生高寬饒節，而非其如此，予書曰：「明主知君潔白公正，不畏強禦，故命君以司察之位，擅君以奉使之權，尊官厚祿已施於君矣。

translations.literal: Wang Sheng, attendant to the heir, admired Kuangrao's integrity but disapproved of his course, and wrote him a letter: "An enlightened ruler knows you are pure, upright, and unafraid of the mighty; therefore he assigned you the office of inspector and gave you the authority of an imperial commissioner; high rank and rich stipend have already been granted you.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Sheng, a tutor to the crown prince, wrote: the emperor knows you are clean and fearless, which is why he gave you the censor's rod and envoy powers—and already pays you like a chief minister.

sentences.zh: 君宜夙夜惟思當世之務，奉法宣化，憂勞天下，雖日有益，月有功，猶未足以稱職而報恩也。

translations.literal: You ought day and night to ponder the tasks of the age, uphold the law and spread moral transformation, and labor with anxiety for the empire—even if you brought profit daily and merit monthly, it would still scarcely suffice to match your office and repay his grace.
translations.idiomatic: He begged Kuangrao to mind the work of the age, enforce the code, and wear himself out for the realm—daily good would still barely repay the trust.

sentences.zh: 自古之治，三王之術各有制度。

translations.literal: From antiquity good order has rested on the three kings' institutions, each with its proper system.
translations.idiomatic: Since high antiquity each sage king had his own institutions.

sentences.zh: 今君不務循職而已，乃欲以太古久遠之事匡拂天子，數進不用難聽之語以摩切左右，非所以揚令名全壽命者也。

translations.literal: Now you do not attend to fulfilling your duties but wish to use remote high-antiquity precedents to correct the Son of Heaven, repeatedly advancing unwelcome and hard-to-hear words to grind against those at his side—this is not the way to raise a fair name or preserve your life.
translations.idiomatic: Instead of doing the job, you lecture the throne with archaic precedents and barbed memorials—neither a way to fame nor to a long life.

sentences.zh: 方今用事之人皆明習法令，言足以飾君之辭，文足以成君之過，君不惟蘧氏之高蹤，而慕子胥之末行，用不訾之軀，臨不測之險，竊為君痛之。

translations.literal: Those in power today are all expert in statutes and orders; their words suffice to polish the ruler's phrases, their documents to complete your faults; if you do not ponder Qu Boyu's high path but imitate Wu Zixu's final conduct, risking an irreplaceable body on unfathomable peril—I privately grieve for you.
translations.idiomatic: Today's ministers know every loophole in the code; they can polish the emperor's prose and pin any fault on you—imitate the safe Qu, not the dead Wu Zixu, or you will gamble a life you cannot replace.

sentences.zh: 夫君子直而不挺，曲而不詘。

translations.literal: The gentleman is straight but not stiff, flexible but not cringing.
translations.idiomatic: The noble man stands straight without snapping, bends without crawling.

sentences.zh: 《大雅》云：『既明且哲，以保其身。』

translations.literal: The "Greater Odes" says: "Clear-sighted and wise, thereby to preserve his person."
translations.idiomatic: The Greater Odes says: "Be bright and wise, and you will keep your skin."

sentences.zh: 狂夫之言，聖人擇焉。

translations.literal: The madman's words—the sage selects from them.
translations.idiomatic: Even a madman's rant may hold a line the sage keeps.

sentences.zh: 唯裁省覽。」

translations.literal: I beg you to weigh and consider this."
translations.idiomatic: Please read this with care."

sentences.zh: 寬饒不納其言。

translations.literal: Kuangrao did not accept his words.
translations.idiomatic: Kuangrao ignored the letter.

sentences.zh: 是時，上方用刑法，信任中尚書宦官，寬饒奏封事曰：「方今聖道浸廢，儒術不行，以刑余為周、召，以法律為《詩》、《書》。」

translations.literal: At that time the ruler relied on punishments and laws, trusted the palace masters of writing and the eunuchs; Kuangrao submitted a sealed memorial saying, "Today the sage way is gradually abandoned, Confucian learning does not prevail; mutilated convicts are treated as the Duke of Zhou and the Duke of Shao, and statutes are taken for the Odes and Documents."
translations.idiomatic: He filed a sealed note: the court treated eunuchs like the Duke of Zhou and the code like the Confucian canon.

sentences.zh: 又引《韓氏易傳》言：「五帝官天下，三王家天下，家以傳子，官以傳賢，若四時之運，功成者去，不得其人則不居其位。」

translations.literal: He also quoted the Han lineage commentary on the Changes: "The Five Thearchs held the empire as a public trust; the three dynastic kings held it as a family possession; families pass it to sons, public trusts to the worthy—it runs like the four seasons; when merit is done the man departs; if the man is not right he does not keep the seat."
translations.idiomatic: He cited the Changes tradition that thearchs abdicated to the worthy while kings handed the throne to sons—like the seasons, merit ends and the seat moves on.

sentences.zh: 書奏，上以寬饒怨謗終不改，下其書中二千石。

translations.literal: When the memorial was submitted, the emperor thought Kuangrao slandered and complained without reform and sent the document down to ministers at two thousand piculs.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor read it as slander and bounced the paper to the high ministers.

sentences.zh: 時，執金吾議，以為寬饒指意欲求禪，大逆不道。

translations.literal: At the time the Bearer of the Mace argued that Kuangrao's meaning was to seek abdication—great treason and lack of the Way.
translations.idiomatic: The Bearer of the Mace read the passage as a call for the emperor to yield the throne—capital treason.

sentences.zh: 諫大夫鄭昌愍傷寬饒忠直憂國，以言事不當意而為文吏所詆挫，上書頌寬饒曰：「臣聞山有猛獸，藜藿為之不採；

translations.literal: Grandee Zheng Chang pitied Kuangrao's loyalty and bluntness and his care for the state, grieving that because his words did not suit the ruler's fancy he was crushed by clerks, and submitted a memorial praising Kuangrao: "I have heard that where mountains hold fierce beasts, brambles and thorns are not gathered;
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Chang wrote: where tigers stalk the hills, no one picks the thorns—

sentences.zh: 國有忠臣，奸邪為之不起。

translations.literal: where the state has loyal ministers, wickedness does not arise.
translations.idiomatic: where the court keeps honest men, treason finds no foothold.

sentences.zh: 司隸校尉寬饒居不求安，食不球飽，進有憂國之心，退有死節之義，上無許、史之屬，下無金、張之托，職在司察，直道而行，多仇少與，上書陳國事，有司劾以大辟，臣幸得從大夫之後，官以諫為名，不敢不言。」

translations.literal: Metropolitan Governor Kuangrao does not seek ease in his dwelling nor fullness in his food; advancing he bears a heart anxious for the state, retiring he keeps the duty to die for principle; he has no patronage from the Xu and Shi clans above nor backing from the Jin and Zhang houses below; his office is inspection, yet he walks the straight path, making many enemies and few friends; he memorializes on state affairs, and the responsible agencies impeach him for a capital crime—I have had the fortune to follow after the grandees and my title is remonstrant; I dare not be silent."
translations.idiomatic: He praised Kuangrao's poverty, courage, and lack of great-clan patrons, then said his own remonstrant's title forced him to speak before the court killed a good officer.

sentences.zh: 上不聽，遂下寬饒吏。

translations.literal: The emperor would not listen and sent Kuangrao down to the clerks.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor refused and handed Kuangrao to the jailers.

sentences.zh: 寬饒引佩刀自剄北闕下，眾莫不憐之。

translations.literal: Kuangrao drew his belt knife and cut his own throat below the northern gate tower; none did not pity him.
translations.idiomatic: He opened his own throat below the northern watchtower; the capital wept.

sentences.zh: 諸葛豐

translations.literal: Zhuge Feng.
translations.idiomatic: Zhuge Feng.

sentences.zh: 諸葛豐字少季，琅邪人也。

translations.literal: Zhuge Feng, courtesy name Shaoji, was a man of Langye.
translations.idiomatic: Zhuge Feng, styled Shaoji, came from Langye.

sentences.zh: 以明經為郡文學，名特立剛直。

translations.literal: On classics examination he became commandery erudite-clerk and was famed for standing alone in integrity and uprightness.
translations.idiomatic: He passed the classics track as literary officer and was known as a lone straight arrow.

sentences.zh: 貢禹為御史大夫，除豐為屬，舉侍御史。

translations.literal: When Gong Yu was imperial counsellor, he appointed Feng as an assistant and recommended him as attendant clerk.
translations.idiomatic: Gong Yu took him on staff and nominated him as palace clerk.

sentences.zh: 元帝擢為司隸校尉，刺舉無所避，京師為之語曰：「間何闊，逢諸葛。」

translations.literal: Emperor Yuan promoted him to metropolitan governor; in impeaching wrongdoing he never swerved, and the capital made a rhyme: "Why so wide a gap—meet Zhuge!"
translations.idiomatic: Yuan made him metropolitan governor; the capital joked, "What blocked the road? Zhuge Feng!"

sentences.zh: 上嘉其節，加豐秩光祿大夫。

translations.literal: The emperor praised his integrity and added Feng's rank to grand coachman of the imperial household.
translations.idiomatic: The court raised his salary to grand coachman for his spine.

sentences.zh: 時，侍中許章以外屬貴幸，奢淫不奉法度，賓客犯事，與章相連。

translations.literal: At the time Gentleman Attendant Xu Zhang, favored as an imperial in-law, was extravagant and dissolute and did not observe the law; retainers committed offenses linked to Zhang.
translations.idiomatic: Xu Zhang the favorite in-law ran wild; his clients broke laws in his name.

sentences.zh: 豐案劾章，欲奉其事，適逢許侍中私出，豐駐車舉節詔章曰：「下！」

translations.literal: Feng built a case impeaching Zhang; wishing to carry the matter through, it happened that Attendant Xu went out privately; Feng halted his carriage, raised his staff, and ordered Zhang, "Dismount!"
translations.idiomatic: Feng tried to arrest Xu Zhang in the street, blocked the carriage, raised his baton, and shouted, "Down from the cart!"

sentences.zh: 欲收之。

translations.literal: He intended to seize him.
translations.idiomatic: He meant to clap him in irons.

sentences.zh: 章迫窘，馳車去，豐追之。

translations.literal: Zhang, pressed and cornered, whipped his carriage away; Feng pursued him.
translations.idiomatic: Xu Zhang lashed his horses; Feng gave chase.

sentences.zh: 許侍中因得入宮門，自歸上。

translations.literal: Attendant Xu therefore reached the palace gate and presented himself to the emperor.
translations.idiomatic: Xu slipped inside the palace gate and threw himself on the emperor's mercy.

sentences.zh: 豐亦上奏，於是收豐節。

translations.literal: Feng also submitted a memorial; thereupon Feng's staff of authority was taken away.
translations.idiomatic: Feng filed his own report; the emperor stripped his baton.

sentences.zh: 司隸去節自豐始。

translations.literal: That the metropolitan governor lost his staff began with Feng.
translations.idiomatic: From Feng onward the capital censor carried no imperial staff.

sentences.zh: 豐上書謝曰：「臣豐駑怯，文不足以勸善，武不足以執邪。

translations.literal: Feng submitted a memorial of apology: "Your subject Feng is dull and timid; in civil matters his words are insufficient to encourage good, in military matters insufficient to seize the wicked.
translations.idiomatic: He wrote a groveling apology: he was too dull for civil or military work.

sentences.zh: 陛下不量臣能否，拜為司隸校尉，未有以自效，復秩臣為光祿大夫，官尊責重，非臣所當處也。

translations.literal: Your Majesty did not measure your subject's ability yet appointed him metropolitan governor; he has had no way to prove himself, and you again raised your subject to grand coachman—office is lofty and responsibility heavy; it is not what your subject should hold.
translations.idiomatic: He said the emperor had overpromoted him to metropolitan governor and then to grand coachman without testing his mettle.

sentences.zh: 又迫年歲衰暮，常恐卒填溝渠，無以報厚德，使論議士譏臣無補，長獲素餐之名。

translations.literal: Moreover, pressed by advancing years, he constantly fears he will suddenly fill a ditch and be unable to repay deep grace, letting debaters mock him as useless and earning forever the name of eating free rice.
translations.idiomatic: He feared dying before he had earned his keep and being mocked as a salary thief.

sentences.zh: 故常願捐一旦之命，不待時而斷奸臣之首，懸於都市，編書其罪，使四方明知為惡之罰，然後卻就斧鉞之誅，誠臣所甘心也。

translations.literal: Therefore he has always wished to throw away a single morning's life, without waiting for the season, to cut off traitors' heads, hang them in the marketplace, inscribe their crimes, so the four quarters clearly know the punishment for evil, and only then accept the axe—this your subject would gladly do.
translations.idiomatic: He swore he would gladly die if he could first spike a traitor's head on the market wall.

sentences.zh: 夫以布衣之士，尚猶有刎頸之交，今以四海之大，曾無伏節死誼之臣，率盡苟合取容，阿黨相為，念私門之利，忘國家之政。

translations.literal: For even among commoners in cloth there are friendships unto death; now within the four seas there are none who die holding their integrity for principle—all follow what is easy to curry favor, form factions for one another, think of private gates' profit, and forget the state's government.
translations.idiomatic: Commoners keep faith unto death while great officers fawn and factionalize, he said—private profit had swallowed public duty.

sentences.zh: 邪穢濁混之氣上感於天，是以災變數見，百姓困乏。

translations.literal: The foul and turbid miasma rises to touch Heaven; therefore portents appear repeatedly and the hundred surnames are exhausted.
translations.idiomatic: That filth had risen to heaven, he argued, which was why omens multiplied and the people starved.

sentences.zh: 此臣下不忠之效也，臣誠恥之亡已。

translations.literal: This is the effect of ministers' disloyalty; your subject is truly shamed beyond end.
translations.idiomatic: He called it proof of ministerial treason and said he was ashamed without end.

sentences.zh: 凡人情莫不欲安存而惡危亡，然忠臣直士不避患害者，誠為君也。

translations.literal: In human feeling none does not wish to live in peace and hate peril and death; yet loyal ministers and straight gentlemen do not flee harm and disaster because they truly act for their ruler.
translations.idiomatic: Honest men risk death because they serve the throne, not themselves.

sentences.zh: 今陛下天覆地載，物無不容，使尚書令堯賜臣豐書曰：『夫司隸者刺舉不法，善善惡惡，非得顓之也。

translations.literal: Now Your Majesty covers and carries all things as heaven and earth; nothing is not contained. You had Master of Writing Yao send your subject Feng a letter saying, 'The metropolitan governor denounces lawlessness, praises good and hates evil—he may not monopolize everything.
translations.idiomatic: He thanked the emperor for a letter from Master of Reading Yao: the censor must praise good and punish evil, not seize every case.

sentences.zh: 勉處中和，順經術意。』

translations.literal: Strive to keep to the mean and harmony and follow the intent of the classics.'
translations.idiomatic: Stay in the middle way and heed the classics.

sentences.zh: 恩深德厚，臣豐頓首幸甚。

translations.literal: The grace is deep and the virtue thick; your subject Feng kowtows in the utmost fortune.
translations.idiomatic: He said the grace overwhelmed him.

sentences.zh: 臣竊不勝憤懣，願賜清宴，唯陛下裁幸。」

translations.literal: Your subject privately cannot overcome his resentment and begs to be granted a clear audience—only let Your Majesty decide."
translations.idiomatic: He begged for a private audience to unburden his grievance."

sentences.zh: 上不許。

translations.literal: The emperor did not grant it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor refused.

sentences.zh: 是後，所言益不用，豐復上書言：「臣聞伯奇孝而棄於親，子胥忠而誅於君，隱公慈而殺於弟，叔武弟而殺于兄。

translations.literal: Afterward his words were ever less heeded; Feng again submitted a memorial saying, "I have heard that Boqi was filial yet cast off by kin, Wu Zixu loyal yet executed by his ruler, Duke Yin merciful yet killed by his younger brother, and Shuwu the younger brother yet killed by his elder brother.
translations.idiomatic: He wrote again listing paragons destroyed by kin or lord—Boqi, Wu Zixu, Duke Yin, Shuwu—

sentences.zh: 夫以四子之行，屈平之材，然猶不能自顯而被刑戮，豈不足以觀哉！

translations.literal: For men of such conduct and Qu Yuan's talent still could not display themselves and met execution—does it not suffice for reflection!
translations.idiomatic: —and asked whether their fate was not warning enough.

sentences.zh: 使臣殺身以安國，蒙誅以顯君，臣誠願之。

translations.literal: If I may kill my body to settle the state and accept execution to clarify the ruler, your subject truly wishes it.
translations.idiomatic: He said he would gladly die to save the dynasty.

sentences.zh: 獨恐未有雲補，而為眾邪所排，令讒夫得遂，正直之路雍塞，忠臣沮心，智士杜口，此愚臣之所懼也。」

translations.literal: I only fear there will be no cloud-bank of help, that I will be thrust aside by a crowd of wicked men, letting slanderers succeed, blocking the road of the straight, disheartening loyal ministers and sealing wise men's mouths—this is what your foolish subject fears."
translations.idiomatic: He feared only that slander would win and honest voices fall silent."

sentences.zh: 豐以春夏系治人，在位多言其短。

translations.literal: Feng arrested and tried people in spring and summer; those in office spoke much of his faults.
translations.idiomatic: He jailed people out of season; the capital gossiped.

sentences.zh: 上徙豐為城門校尉，豐上書告光祿勳周堪、光祿大夫張猛。

translations.literal: The emperor transferred Feng to colonel of the city gates; Feng submitted a memorial accusing Chamberlain for the Palace Garrison Zhou Kan and grandee of the palace Zhang Meng.
translations.idiomatic: Demoted to gate colonel, he struck back with charges against Zhou Kan and Zhang Meng.

sentences.zh: 上不直豐，乃制詔御史：「城門校尉豐，前與光祿勳堪、光祿大夫猛在朝之時，數稱言堪、猛之美。

translations.literal: The emperor did not side with Feng and therefore ordered the imperial secretary by edict: "Colonel of the city gates Feng, earlier when together in court with Chamberlain for the Palace Garrison Kan and Grandee of the Palace Meng, repeatedly praised Kan and Meng's excellence.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor's edict said Feng had once praised Zhou Kan and Zhang Meng.

sentences.zh: 豐前為司隸校尉，不順四時，修法度，專作苛暴，以獲虛威，朕不忍下吏，以為城門校尉。

translations.literal: When Feng was metropolitan governor he did not follow the four seasons or repair standards of law but acted solely harsh and violent to win empty awe; We could not bear to send him to clerks and made him colonel of the city gates.
translations.idiomatic: It recalled his harsh tenure as censor and said the throne had spared him a jail term by demoting him to the gates.

sentences.zh: 不內省諸己。

translations.literal: He did not look within to examine himself.
translations.idiomatic: He had never searched his own conscience.

sentences.zh: 而反怨堪、猛，以求報舉，告案無證之辭，暴揚難驗之罪，毀譽恣意，不顧前言，不信之大者也。

translations.literal: Instead he resents Kan and Meng, seeking revenge for their recommendations, advancing charges without evidence, exposing crimes hard to verify, slandering and praising at will, heedless of his earlier words—this is extreme untrustworthiness.
translations.idiomatic: Now he smeared the same men he had praised—evidence of bad faith.

sentences.zh: 朕憐豐之耆老，不忍加刑，其免為庶人。」

translations.literal: We pity Feng's old age and cannot bear to add corporal punishment; let him be dismissed as a commoner."
translations.idiomatic: The edict spared the cane and stripped him to commoner status."

sentences.zh: 終於家。

translations.literal: He ended his life at home.
translations.idiomatic: He died at home.

sentences.zh: 劉輔，河間宗室人也。

translations.literal: Liu Fu was a man of the imperial house of Hejian.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Fu belonged to the Hejian royal lineage.

sentences.zh: 舉孝廉，為襄賁令。

translations.literal: Recommended as filial and incorrupt, he became magistrate of Xiangben.
translations.idiomatic: A filial-incorrupt nomination made him magistrate of Xiangben.

sentences.zh: 上書言得失，召見，上美其材，擢為諫大夫。

translations.literal: He submitted a memorial on gains and losses, was summoned for audience, and the emperor praised his talent and promoted him to grandee remonstrant.
translations.idiomatic: A policy memorial won him an audience and a jump to grandee remonstrant.

sentences.zh: 會成帝欲立趙婕妤為皇后，先下詔封婕妤父臨為列侯。

translations.literal: It happened that Emperor Cheng wished to establish the Honorable Lady Zhao as empress and first issued an edict enfeoffing her father Lin as a full marquis.
translations.idiomatic: Chengdi meant to raise Zhao Feiyan to empress and first titled her father a marquis.

sentences.zh: 輔上書言：「臣聞天之所與，必先賜以符瑞；

translations.literal: Fu submitted a memorial saying, "I have heard that what Heaven favors it first endows with talismanic omens;
translations.idiomatic: Liu Fu wrote: Heaven's favor shows first in omens—

sentences.zh: 天之所違，必先降以災變：此神明之徵應，自然之占驗也。

translations.literal: what Heaven rejects it first strikes with disaster and anomaly: this is the spirits' response and nature's proof.
translations.idiomatic: —Heaven's anger shows first in disasters; that is how the spirits speak.

sentences.zh: 昔武王、周公承順天地，以饗魚烏之瑞，然猶君臣祗懼，動色相戒，況於季世，不蒙繼嗣之福，屢受威怒之異者虖！

translations.literal: Formerly King Wu and the Duke of Zhou obeyed heaven and earth and received the fish-and-crow omens, yet lord and minister still trembled in fear and warned one another with changed countenance—how much more in a declining age that does not receive heir-fortune and repeatedly suffers awesome wrath in portents!
translations.idiomatic: Even Wu and Zhou trembled at small omens, he argued—how much more should a childless age fear heaven's warnings.

sentences.zh: 雖夙夜自責，改過易行，畏天命，念祖業，妙選有德之世，考卜窈窕之女，以承宗廟，順神祗心，塞天下望，子孫之詳猶恐晚暮，今乃觸情縱欲，傾於卑賤之女，欲以母天下，不畏於天，不愧於人，惑莫大焉。

translations.literal: Though one reproaches oneself morning and night, reforms faults and changes conduct, fears heaven's command, remembers ancestral enterprise, finely selects a house of virtue, divines for a modest maiden to continue the ancestral temple, and obeys the spirits' hearts to stop the empire's hopes—even then good news of heirs may come too late; now you give way to passion and desire, incline toward a woman of base birth, and wish her to mother the world—without fearing heaven or shame before men—no delusion is greater.
translations.idiomatic: He begged the emperor not to make a dancing-girl empress when the altars still lacked an heir.

sentences.zh: 里語曰：『腐木不可以為柱，卑人不可以為主。』

translations.literal: The village proverb says, 'Rotten wood cannot be a pillar; a base person cannot be ruler.'
translations.idiomatic: Village wisdom says rotten timber cannot be a ridgepole and a lowborn woman cannot mother the realm.

sentences.zh: 天人之所不予，必有禍而無福，市道皆共知之，朝廷莫肯一言，臣竊傷心。

translations.literal: What heaven and men withhold brings calamity without blessing; the marketplace knows it in common, yet the court will not speak one word—I privately grieve.
translations.idiomatic: He said every market knew the match was ill-omened while the court stayed silent.

sentences.zh: 自念得以同姓拔擢，尸祿不忠，污辱諫爭之官，不敢不盡死，唯陛下深察。」

translations.literal: Reflecting that I was raised because I share the surname, I have eaten salary without loyalty and shamed the remonstrant's office—I dare not fail to exhaust death; I beg Your Majesty to examine deeply."
translations.idiomatic: He said his clan tie obliged him to speak even at the cost of his life."

sentences.zh: 書奏，上使侍御史收縛輔，系掖庭秘獄，群臣莫知其故。

translations.literal: When the memorial was submitted, the emperor had an attendant clerk seize and bind Fu and confined him in the secret prison of the Rear Palace; none of the ministers knew the reason.
translations.idiomatic: Chengdi jailed him in the harem's secret jail without telling the ministers why.

sentences.zh: 於是中朝左將軍辛慶忌、右將軍廉褒、光祿勳師丹、太中大夫谷永俱上書曰：「臣聞明王垂寬容之聽，崇諫爭之官，廣開忠直之路，不罪狂狷之言，然後百僚在位，竭忠盡謀，不懼後患，朝廷無諂諛之士，元首無失道之愆。

translations.literal: Thereupon Left General Xin Qingji, Right General Lian Bao, Chamberlain for Ceremonies Shi Dan, and Grandee of the Palace Gu Yong jointly submitted a memorial saying, "We have heard that an enlightened king extends a tolerant ear, honors remonstrance officers, opens wide the road of loyalty and uprightness, and does not punish wild or blunt words—then the hundred officials hold their posts, exhaust loyalty and counsel, fear no later disaster, the court has no flatterers, and the sovereign incurs no fault of losing the Way.
translations.idiomatic: Four senior ministers wrote that wise rulers welcome blunt counsel and never jail remonstrants.

sentences.zh: 竊見諫大夫劉輔，前以縣令求見，擢為諫大夫，此其言必有卓詭切至，當聖心者，故得拔至於此。

translations.literal: We have observed Grandee Liu Fu, who earlier as county magistrate sought audience and was raised to grandee remonstrant—his words must have been striking and cutting to the sacred mind, therefore he was pulled up to this rank.
translations.idiomatic: They said Liu Fu must have touched a nerve to rise so fast from county magistrate.

sentences.zh: 旬日之間，收下秘獄，臣等愚，以為輔幸得托公族之親，在諫臣之列，新從下土來，未知朝廷體，獨觸忌諱，不足深過。

translations.literal: Within ten days he was taken to the secret prison; we are foolish and think that Fu, fortunate to claim kinship with the ducal house and stand in the remonstrants' ranks, newly come from the local level and not yet knowing court etiquette, merely brushed a taboo and does not deserve deep blame.
translations.idiomatic: They pleaded ignorance of court taboo for a newcomer from the provinces.

sentences.zh: 小罪宜隱忍而已，如有大惡，宜暴治理官，與眾共之。

translations.literal: Small faults should be borne with silently; if there were great evil, it should be exposed in open trial and shared with the multitude.
translations.idiomatic: Small slips deserved patience; capital crimes deserved a public trial.

sentences.zh: 昔趙簡子殺其大夫鳴犢，孔子臨河而還。

translations.literal: Formerly Zhao Jianzi executed his minister Mingdu, and Confucius turned back from the river.
translations.idiomatic: They cited Confucius turning back from the Yellow River when Zhao Jianzi killed his worthy.

sentences.zh: 今天心未豫，災異屢降，水旱迭臻，方當隆寬廣問，褒直盡下之時也。

translations.literal: Now heaven's mind is not pleased, portents descend repeatedly, flood and drought come in turn—this is precisely the time to widen mercy, ask broadly, and praise the straight who exhaust words from below.
translations.idiomatic: With omens piling up, they said, the throne should widen its ear, not narrow it.

sentences.zh: 而行慘急之誅於諫爭之臣，震驚群下，失忠直心。

translations.literal: Yet you apply cruel and hasty execution to a remonstrance officer, shocking those below and losing the hearts of the loyal and straight.
translations.idiomatic: Jailing a remonstrant would terrify every honest voice in the empire.

sentences.zh: 假令輔不坐直言，所坐不著，天下不可戶曉。

translations.literal: Suppose Fu is not convicted for blunt speech and the charge does not stand clear—the empire cannot be informed house by house.
translations.idiomatic: If the charge were murky, rumor would poison trust from gate to gate.

sentences.zh: 同姓近臣本以言顯，其於治親養忠之義誠不宜幽囚於掖庭獄。

translations.literal: A kinsman and close minister is raised by his words; for the meaning of ordering kin and nurturing loyalty he truly should not be hidden in the Rear Palace prison.
translations.idiomatic: A royal remonstrant should not rot in a harem lockup.

sentences.zh: 公卿以下見陛下進用輔亟，而折傷之暴，人有懼心，精銳銷耎，莫敢盡節正言，非所以昭有虞之聽，廣德美之風也。

translations.literal: From the high ministers downward, seeing Your Majesty promote Fu swiftly yet break him so violently, people harbor fear, keen edge melts to softness, and none dares exhaust integrity in straight speech—this is not the way to display the listening of Shun or widen the wind of virtuous beauty.
translations.idiomatic: They warned that silencing Liu Fu would teach every official to hold his tongue.

sentences.zh: 臣等竊深傷之，唯陛下留神省察。」

translations.literal: We your subjects privately grieve deeply; we beg Your Majesty to give careful thought and examination."
translations.idiomatic: They begged the emperor to reconsider."

sentences.zh: 上乃徙系輔共工獄，減死罪一等，論為鬼薪。

translations.literal: The emperor thereupon moved Fu's confinement to the Gonggong prison, reduced capital crime by one degree, and sentenced him to convict labor as "ghost firewood."
translations.idiomatic: Chengdi shifted him to a lesser jail and commuted the sentence to convict logging.

sentences.zh: 終於家。

translations.literal: He ended his life at home.
translations.idiomatic: He finished his life in private.

sentences.zh: 鄭崇字子游，本高密大族，世與王家相嫁娶。

translations.literal: Zheng Chong, courtesy name Ziyou, was originally from a great clan of Gaomi and for generations intermarried with the Wang family.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Chong of Gaomi was tied by marriage to the Wang clan for generations.

sentences.zh: 祖父以訾徙平陵。

translations.literal: His grandfather moved to Pingling on wealth rating.
translations.idiomatic: His grandfather bought resettlement at Pingling.

sentences.zh: 父賓明法令，為御史，事貢公，名公直。

translations.literal: His father Bin understood statutes and orders, served as imperial clerk, attended Lord Gong, and was famed for public uprightness.
translations.idiomatic: His father served Gong Yu as a clerk famed for straight dealing.

sentences.zh: 崇少為郡文學史，至丞相大車屬。

translations.literal: In youth Chong was commandery literary clerk and rose to carriage attendant under the chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: He rose from literary clerk to the chancellor's carriage office.

sentences.zh: 弟立與高武侯傅喜同門學，相友善。

translations.literal: His younger brother Li studied in the same school as Marquis of Gaowu Fu Xi and they were close friends.
translations.idiomatic: His brother Zheng Li was schoolmates with Fu Xi.

sentences.zh: 喜為大司馬，薦崇，哀帝擢為尚書僕射。

translations.literal: When Xi became grand marshal he recommended Chong, and Emperor Ai promoted him to vice director of the masters of writing.
translations.idiomatic: Fu Xi's grand marshalship lifted Zheng Chong to vice president of the secretariat under Ai.

sentences.zh: 數求見諫爭，上初納用之。

translations.literal: He repeatedly sought audience to remonstrate; at first the emperor accepted and used him.
translations.idiomatic: At first Ai listened to his blunt visits.

sentences.zh: 每見曳革履，上笑曰：「我識鄭尚書履聲。」

translations.literal: Whenever he was seen dragging leather sandals, the emperor laughed and said, "I recognize Minister Zheng's shoe sound."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor joked that he could pick Zheng's tread out of any corridor.

sentences.zh: 久之，上欲封祖母傅太后從弟商，崇諫曰：「孝成皇帝封親舅五侯，天為赤黃晝昏，日中有黑氣。

translations.literal: After a long while the emperor wished to enfeoff his grandmother Empress Dowager Fu's cousin Shang; Chong remonstrated: "Emperor Cheng enfeoffed five maternal uncles as marquises; heaven turned red-yellow and noon grew dark, and black vapor appeared in the sun.
translations.idiomatic: He cited Chengdi's five uncle marquis enfeoffments and the sun turning black.

sentences.zh: 今祖母從昆弟二人已侯。

translations.literal: Now two elder cousins on the grandmother's side are already marquises.
translations.idiomatic: Two Fu cousins already held marquisates.

sentences.zh: 孔鄉侯，皇后父；

translations.literal: The Marquis of Kongxiang is the empress's father;
translations.idiomatic: One was the Zhao empress's father—

sentences.zh: 高武侯以三公封，尚有因緣。

translations.literal: the Marquis of Gaowu was enfeoffed from the three dukes' rank and still had a proper rationale.
translations.idiomatic: —the other had risen through the three dukes and still looked defensible.

sentences.zh: 今無故欲復封商，壞亂制度，逆天人之心，非傅氏之福也。

translations.literal: Now without cause you wish again to enfeoff Shang, wrecking the system, reversing heaven and men's hearts—it is not the Fu family's blessing.
translations.idiomatic: A third Fu marquis with no excuse would break precedent and anger heaven, he said.

sentences.zh: 臣聞師曰：『逆陽者厥極弱，逆陰者厥極凶短折，犯人者有亂亡之患，犯神者有疾夭之禍。』

translations.literal: I have heard the teacher say, 'Whoever reverses yang reaches the extreme of weakness; whoever reverses yin reaches the extreme of violent early death; whoever offends men has the peril of rebellion and loss; whoever offends the spirits meets sickness and untimely death.'
translations.idiomatic: He quoted masters on the price of reversing yin and yang.

sentences.zh: 故周公著戒曰：『惟王不知艱難，唯耽樂是從，時亦罔有克壽。』

translations.literal: Therefore the Duke of Zhou wrote the warning, 'If the king does not know hardship and follows only dissolute pleasure, then too there will be none who reach old age.'
translations.idiomatic: He quoted Zhou's warning against pleasure-drunk kings.

sentences.zh: 故衰世之君夭折蚤沒，此皆犯陰之害也。

translations.literal: Therefore declining-age rulers die young and perish early—all are harms from offending yin.
translations.idiomatic: He said short-lived emperors paid for yin excess.

sentences.zh: 臣願以身命當國咎。」

translations.literal: Your subject is willing to answer for the state's fault with body and life."
translations.idiomatic: He offered his own life to cancel the omen."

sentences.zh: 崇因持詔書案起。

translations.literal: Thereupon Chong seized the edict document and rose from his seat.
translations.idiomatic: He snatched the edict scroll and stood up in open defiance.

sentences.zh: 傅太后大怒曰：「何有為天子乃反為一臣所顓制邪！」

translations.literal: Empress Dowager Fu raged and said, "How can there be a Son of Heaven yet be wholly controlled by one minister!"
translations.idiomatic: Grand Dowager Fu screamed that a minister must not own the emperor.

sentences.zh: 上遂下詔曰：「朕幼而孤，皇太太后躬自養育，免於襁褓，教道以禮，至於成人，惠澤茂焉。

translations.literal: The emperor thereupon issued an edict saying, "We were orphaned young; the great imperial grandmother personally reared Us, saving Us from swaddling clothes, teaching Us with ritual until adulthood—her favor is lush.
translations.idiomatic: Ai's edict rehearsed how the Fu grand empress had raised him from infancy.

sentences.zh: 『欲報之德，昊天罔極。』

translations.literal: 'The virtue I would repay—vast heaven has no limit.'
translations.idiomatic: He quoted the Odes on boundless debt to a parent.

sentences.zh: 前追號皇太太后父為崇祖侯，惟念德報未殊，朕甚恧焉。

translations.literal: Earlier We posthumously titled the great imperial grandmother's father as Marquis of Chongzu, yet thinking the reward for virtue was not special, We are greatly ashamed.
translations.idiomatic: He said earlier honors to the Fu line still felt inadequate.

sentences.zh: 侍中光祿大夫商，皇太太后父同產子，小自保大，恩義最親。

translations.literal: Gentleman attendant and grandee of the palace Shang, a son born of the same womb as the great imperial grandmother's father, was raised small to great and is closest in grace and duty.
translations.idiomatic: Fu Shang was the grand empress's half-brother and the nearest kin left.

sentences.zh: 其封商為汝昌侯，為崇祖侯後，更號崇祖侯為汝昌哀侯。」

translations.literal: Enfeoff Shang as marquis of Ruchang, to continue the Marquis of Chongzu; change the title of Marquis of Chongzu to Marquis of Ruchang mournful."
translations.idiomatic: The edict enfeoffed Fu Shang at Ruchang and rewrote the older shrine title."

sentences.zh: 崇又以董賢貴寵過度諫，由是重得罪。

translations.literal: Chong again remonstrated because Dong Xian's favor was excessive and thereby incurred heavier guilt.
translations.idiomatic: He crossed the emperor again over Dong Xian's favor and paid for it.

sentences.zh: 數以職事見責，發疾頸癰，欲乞骸骨，不敢。

translations.literal: Repeatedly blamed in office matters, he developed a neck abscess, wished to beg bones for retirement, but dared not.
translations.idiomatic: Office politics gave him a neck ulcer; he dared not ask to retire.

sentences.zh: 尚書令趙昌佞諂，素害崇，知其見疏，因奏崇與宗族通，疑有奸，請治。

translations.literal: Master of Writing Zhao Chang was sycophantic and long hated Chong; knowing Chong was out of favor, he memorialized that Chong had improper dealings with his lineage and requested trial.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Chang struck when Zheng was out of favor, alleging clan conspiracy.

sentences.zh: 上責崇曰：「君門如市人，何以欲禁切主上？」

translations.literal: The emperor reproached Chong, saying, "Your gate is like a market of men—why do you wish to restrain the ruler?"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor sneered that his doorway looked like a bazaar.

sentences.zh: 崇對曰：「臣門如市，臣心如水，願得考覆。」

translations.literal: Chong replied, "My gate may be like a market; my heart is like water—I beg to be investigated."
translations.idiomatic: Zheng answered, "The gate is busy; the heart is still as water—investigate me."

sentences.zh: 上怒，下崇獄，窮治，死獄中。

translations.literal: The emperor, angry, sent Chong to prison; he was pursued to the limit and died in jail.
translations.idiomatic: Ai jailed him until he died under interrogation.

sentences.zh: 孫寶字子嚴，穎川鄢陵人也，以明經為郡吏。

translations.literal: Sun Bao, courtesy name Ziyan, was a man of Yanling in Yingchuan; on classics examination he became a commandery clerk.
translations.idiomatic: Sun Bao of Yingchuan entered service through the classics.

sentences.zh: 御史大夫張忠辟寶為屬，欲令授子經，更為除捨，設儲偫。

translations.literal: Imperial Counsellor Zhang Zhong summoned Bao as an assistant, wished to have him teach his son the classics, prepared another residence for him, and set out stores.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Zhong wanted him to tutor his son and built him a private lodging.

sentences.zh: 寶自劾去，忠固還之，心內不平。

translations.literal: Bao impeached himself to leave; Zhong firmly recalled him, but inwardly was displeased.
translations.idiomatic: Sun Bao resigned on principle; Zhang Zhong recalled him but nursed a grudge.

sentences.zh: 後署寶主簿，寶徙入捨，祭灶請比鄰。

translations.literal: Later he assigned Bao as chief clerk; Bao moved into the residence, sacrificed to the stove, and invited the neighbors.
translations.idiomatic: Sun Bao took the chief-clerk post, sacrificed to the kitchen god, and invited the neighbors in.

sentences.zh: 忠陰察，怪之，使所親問寶：「前大夫為君設除大捨，子自劾去者，欲為高節也。

translations.literal: Zhong secretly observed, thought it strange, and had someone close ask Bao, "Earlier the counsellor prepared a great residence for you, yet you impeached yourself to leave—did you wish to show lofty integrity?
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Zhong sent a spy to ask why he first refused the grand lodging.

sentences.zh: 今兩府高士俗不為主簿，子既為之，徙捨甚說，何前後不相副也？」

translations.literal: Now both offices esteem high gentlemen and customarily do not take the chief clerk post; you have taken it, yet moving quarters pleased you greatly—how does this match your earlier conduct?"
translations.idiomatic: The question noted that highborn men usually scorn chief clerk—yet Sun Bao now seemed happy in the job.

sentences.zh: 寶曰：「高士不為主簿，而大夫君以寶為可，一府莫言非，士安得獨自高？

translations.literal: Bao said, "High gentlemen do not take the chief clerk post, yet if the counsellor thinks Bao fit and no one in the bureau objects, how can a gentleman alone hold himself aloof?
translations.idiomatic: Sun Bao replied that if the chancellor trusted him, pride was vanity.

sentences.zh: 前日君男欲學文，而移寶自近。

translations.literal: The other day your lord's son wished to study letters, and you moved Bao close by.
translations.idiomatic: The son had wanted tutoring, so the move served the boy.

sentences.zh: 禮有來學，義無往教；

translations.literal: By ritual there is coming to learn; there is no going to teach;
translations.idiomatic: Ritual says pupils come to the teacher, not the reverse—

sentences.zh: 道不可詘，身詘何傷？

translations.literal: the Way cannot be bent—what harm if the person bends?
translations.idiomatic: —and the Way does not bend for convenience.

sentences.zh: 且不遭者可無不為，況主簿乎！」

translations.literal: Moreover, when one has not encountered refusal, one may do anything—how much more the chief clerk!"
translations.idiomatic: Besides, he said, a chief clerk was hardly beneath him."

sentences.zh: 忠聞之，甚慚，上書薦寶經明質直，宜備近臣。

translations.literal: When Zhong heard this he was deeply ashamed and submitted a memorial recommending Bao as clear in classics, upright in substance, and fit to serve near the throne.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Zhong apologized with a recommendation to the court.

sentences.zh: 為議郎，遷諫大夫。

translations.literal: He became deliberation gentleman and was promoted to grandee remonstrant.
translations.idiomatic: He rose to deliberation gentleman, then remonstrant.

sentences.zh: 鴻嘉中，廣漢群盜起，選為益州刺史。

translations.literal: In the Hongjia era bandits rose in Guanghan; he was selected governor of Yi Province.
translations.idiomatic: Hongjia banditry won him the Yi Province governorship.

sentences.zh: 廣漢太守扈商者，大司馬車騎將軍王音姊子，軟弱不任職。

translations.literal: Governor of Guanghan Hu Shang was the nephew of Grand Marshal and chariot-and-cavalry general Wang Yin—soft and weak, unfit for office.
translations.idiomatic: The Guanghan governor was Wang Yin's weak nephew Hu Shang.

sentences.zh: 寶到部，親入山谷，諭告群盜，非本造意。

translations.literal: When Bao reached his inspectorate he personally entered the mountain valleys, announced and explained to the bandit hosts that they had not originated the plot.
translations.idiomatic: Sun Bao hiked into the hills and told bands they had been misled.

sentences.zh: 渠率皆得悔過自出，遣歸田裡。

translations.literal: The chiefs all obtained leave to repent and come out, and he sent them home to their fields.
translations.idiomatic: Chieftains surrendered and he sent them back to their farms.

sentences.zh: 自劾矯制，奏商為亂首，《春秋》之義，誅首惡而已。

translations.literal: He impeached himself for exceeding authority by edict and memorialized that Shang was chief author of the disorder—by Spring and Autumn principle only the worst offender should be punished.
translations.idiomatic: He confessed to bending an edict while asking that Hu Shang bear the blame as ringleader.

sentences.zh: 商亦奏寶所縱或有渠率當坐者。

translations.literal: Shang also memorialized that among those Bao released some chiefs ought still to be liable.
translations.idiomatic: Hu Shang struck back, claiming some freed men deserved death.

sentences.zh: 商徵下獄，寶坐失死罪免。

translations.literal: Shang was summoned and imprisoned; Bao was convicted for letting a capital crime slip and dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: Hu Shang went to jail; Sun Bao fell for sparing capital offenders.

sentences.zh: 益州吏民多陳寶功效，言為車騎將軍所排。

translations.literal: Yi Province clerks and people repeatedly stated Bao's achievements, saying he had been squeezed out by the chariot-and-cavalry general.
translations.idiomatic: Yi locals petitioned that Wang Yin had railroaded him.

sentences.zh: 上復拜寶為冀州刺史，遷丞相司直。

translations.literal: The emperor again appointed Bao governor of Ji Province and promoted him to straightener for the chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: The court recalled him to Ji Province, then to the chancellor's inspectorate.

sentences.zh: 徵為京兆尹。

translations.literal: He was summoned to be metropolitan governor.
translations.idiomatic: Next he took the capital governorship.

sentences.zh: 故吏侯文以剛直不苟合，常稱疾不肯仕，寶以恩禮請文，欲為布衣友，日設酒食，妻子相對。

translations.literal: His former subordinate Hou Wen was rigidly upright and would not compromise; he often pleaded illness and refused office; Bao with kindness and ritual invited Wen, wishing to be plain-clothes friends with him, daily setting out wine and food while wives and children faced one another.
translations.idiomatic: He courted his old clerk Hou Wen with feasts and treated him as an equal.

sentences.zh: 文求受署為掾，進見如賓禮。

translations.literal: Wen begged to receive appointment as clerk and on entering audience was received with guest ritual.
translations.idiomatic: Hou Wen accepted a clerkship and got guest-of-honor treatment.

sentences.zh: 數月，以立秋日署文東部督郵。

translations.literal: After several months, on the Beginning of Autumn day he appointed Wen eastern-circuit surveillance inspector.
translations.idiomatic: On Beginning of Autumn he named Hou Wen eastern circuit inspector.

sentences.zh: 入見，敕曰：「今日鷹隼始擊，當順天氣取奸惡，以成嚴霜之誅，掾部渠有其人乎？」

translations.literal: When Wen entered audience, Bao charged him, saying, "Today the hawk and falcon first strike—one should follow heaven's qi to take the wicked and complete the frost's execution; does your circuit have such a man?"
translations.idiomatic: Sun Bao asked whether his district held a villain fit for autumn justice.

sentences.zh: 文卬曰：「無其人不敢空受職。」

translations.literal: Wen looked up and said, "If there is none I dare not accept the office empty."
translations.idiomatic: Hou Wen said he would not take an empty commission.

sentences.zh: 寶曰：「誰也？」

translations.literal: Bao said, "Who is it?"
translations.idiomatic: "Whom do you mean?" asked Sun Bao.

sentences.zh: 文曰：「霸陵杜稚季。」

translations.literal: Wen said, "Du Zhiji of Baling."
translations.idiomatic: "Du Zhiji of Baling," said Hou Wen.

sentences.zh: 寶曰：「其次？」

translations.literal: Bao said, "Who next?"
translations.idiomatic: "Who else?" asked Sun Bao.

sentences.zh: 文曰：「豺狼橫道，不宜復問狐狸。」

translations.literal: Wen said, "When wolves block the road one should not ask about foxes."
translations.idiomatic: "When wolves sit the road," Hou Wen said, "you do not hunt foxes."

sentences.zh: 寶默然。

translations.literal: Bao was silent.
translations.idiomatic: Sun Bao said nothing.

sentences.zh: 稚季者大俠，與衛尉淳于長、大鴻臚蕭育等皆厚善。

translations.literal: Zhiji was a great bravo, thick with Commandant of the Guards Chunyu Zhang and grand herald Xiao Yu.
translations.idiomatic: Du Zhiji was tied to Chunyu Zhang and Xiao Yu.

sentences.zh: 寶前失車騎將軍，與紅陽侯有隙，自恐見危，時淳于長方貴幸，友寶，寶亦欲附之，始視事而長以稚季托寶，故寶窮，無以復應文。

translations.literal: Bao had earlier lost the chariot-and-cavalry general and had a rift with the Marquis of Hongyang; fearing danger for himself, at the time Chunyu Zhang was newly favored and befriended Bao, and Bao also wished to attach to him—just as he took office Zhang entrusted Zhiji to Bao, so Bao was cornered and had no way to answer Wen.
translations.idiomatic: Sun Bao feared Hongyang and needed Chunyu Zhang—who had just asked him to spare Du Zhiji—so he could not touch Hou Wen's target.

sentences.zh: 文怪寶氣索，知其有故，因曰：「明府素著威名，今下敢取稚季，當且闔閣，勿有所問。

translations.literal: Wen marveled that Bao's spirit had drained and knew there was a reason; therefore he said, "The governor has always borne a fearsome name; now if you dare take Zhiji, you should for the moment shut your gate and ask nothing else.
translations.idiomatic: Hou Wen offered a face-saving out: ignore Du for a year and no one would call the governor a coward.

sentences.zh: 如此竟歲，吏民未敢誣明府也。

translations.literal: Thus a full year passes and clerks and people still dare not slander the governor.
translations.idiomatic: A quiet year would restore the governor's name.

sentences.zh: 即度稚季而譴它事，眾口言雚嘩，終身自墮。」

translations.literal: Only after marking Zhiji should you press other matters—otherwise popular clamor will rise and you will ruin yourself for life."
translations.idiomatic: Move on other cases only after Du fell, he warned, or the city would howl Sun Bao down."

sentences.zh: 寶曰：「受教。」

translations.literal: Bao said, "I accept your teaching."
translations.idiomatic: Sun Bao said, "Understood."

sentences.zh: 稚季耳目長，聞知之，杜門不通水火，穿捨後牆為小戶，但持鋤自治園，因文所厚自陳如此。

translations.literal: Zhiji's eyes and ears were long; hearing this, he barred his gate, cut off fire and water, dug a small door through the back wall of his compound, and with a hoe alone tended his garden, explaining through Wen's confidants that he did so.
translations.idiomatic: Du Zhiji walled himself in, bored a postern, and hoed his garden until Hou Wen vouched for him.

sentences.zh: 文曰：「我與稚季幸同土壤，素無睚眥，顧受將命，分當相直。

translations.literal: Wen said, "I share soil with Zhiji by fortune and have never borne a grudge; I only received the general's order and must do my duty.
translations.idiomatic: Hou Wen said he bore Du no malice but had orders.

sentences.zh: 誠能自改，嚴將不治前事，即不更心，但更門戶，適趣禍耳。」

translations.literal: If he can truly reform, the stern general will not revisit earlier deeds; if he will not change his heart but only changes his gate, he is merely courting disaster."
translations.idiomatic: Reform would win mercy; hiding would bring worse trouble."

sentences.zh: 稚季遂不敢犯法，寶亦竟歲無所譴。

translations.literal: Zhiji then dared not break the law, and Bao through the whole year impeached no one.
translations.idiomatic: Du kept clean; Sun Bao brought no case that year.

sentences.zh: 明年，稚季病死。

translations.literal: The next year Zhiji died of illness.
translations.idiomatic: Du Zhiji died the next winter.

sentences.zh: 寶為京兆尹三歲，京師稱之。

translations.literal: Bao was metropolitan governor three years; the capital praised him.
translations.idiomatic: Three years in the capital won praise.

sentences.zh: 會淳于長敗，寶與蕭育等皆坐免官。

translations.literal: When Chunyu Zhang fell, Bao and Xiao Yu and others were all dismissed for implication.
translations.idiomatic: Chunyu Zhang's fall swept Sun Bao and Xiao Yu out with him.

sentences.zh: 文復去吏，死於家。

translations.literal: Wen again left office and died at home.
translations.idiomatic: Hou Wen went home and died.

sentences.zh: 稚季子杜蒼，字君敖，名出稚季右，在遊俠中。

translations.literal: Zhiji's son Du Cang, courtesy name Jun'ao, had a name outshining Zhiji's and ranked among the wandering bravos.
translations.idiomatic: Du Cang eclipsed his father's fame in the bravo lists.

sentences.zh: 哀帝即位，徵寶為諫大夫，遷司隸。

translations.literal: When Emperor Ai ascended, Bao was summoned as grandee remonstrant and promoted to metropolitan governor.
translations.idiomatic: Ai recalled him to censor, then metropolitan governor.

sentences.zh: 初，傅太后與中山孝王母馮太后俱事元帝，有隙，傅太后使有司考馮太后，令自殺，眾庶冤之。

translations.literal: Earlier Empress Dowager Fu and Empress Dowager Feng of Zhongshan filial king had both served Emperor Yuan and bore a rift; Empress Dowager Fu had responsible agencies investigate Empress Dowager Feng, ordering her to kill herself—the multitude thought it unjust.
translations.idiomatic: Fu's faction had forced Lady Feng of Zhongshan to suicide; opinion called it a frame.

sentences.zh: 寶奏請覆治，傅太后大怒，曰：「帝置司隸，主使察我。

translations.literal: Bao memorialized requesting reinvestigation; Empress Dowager Fu raged and said, "The emperor set up the metropolitan governor to spy on me!
translations.idiomatic: Sun Bao asked to reopen the case; Grand Dowager Fu said the censor was spying on her.

sentences.zh: 馮氏反事明白，故欲□觖以揚我惡。

translations.literal: The Feng affair was already clear; therefore he wishes to pick at faults and broadcast my evil.
translations.idiomatic: She said the Feng case was closed and Sun Bao only meant to smear her.

sentences.zh: 我當坐之。」

translations.literal: I shall sit in judgment on him."
translations.idiomatic: "I will try him myself," she said.

sentences.zh: 上乃順指下寶獄。

translations.literal: The emperor thereupon followed her hint and sent Bao down to prison.
translations.idiomatic: Ai folded and jailed Sun Bao.

sentences.zh: 尚書僕射唐林爭之，上以林朋黨比周，左遷敦煌魚澤障候。

translations.literal: Vice director of the masters of writing Tang Lin disputed it; the emperor took Lin for factional clique-making and demoted him to warden of the Yuzhi barrier at Dunhuang.
translations.idiomatic: Tang Lin defended Sun Bao and was demoted to a desert outpost.

sentences.zh: 大司馬傅喜、光祿大夫龔勝固爭，上為言太后，出寶復官。

translations.literal: Grand marshal Fu Xi and grandee of the palace Gong Sheng argued firmly; the emperor spoke for Bao to the empress dowager and released Bao to resume office.
translations.idiomatic: Fu Xi and Gong Sheng forced a compromise through the empress dowager.

sentences.zh: 頃之，鄭崇下獄，寶上書曰：「臣聞疏不圖親，外不慮內。

translations.literal: Before long Zheng Chong was imprisoned; Bao submitted a memorial saying, "I have heard that the distant do not plot against kin, the outsider does not scheme against the inner.
translations.idiomatic: When Zheng Chong fell, Sun Bao wrote that outsiders should not judge inner-palace kin.

sentences.zh: 臣幸得銜命奉使，職在刺舉，不敢避貴幸之勢，以塞視聽之明。

translations.literal: Your subject has had the fortune to bear commission and serve in inspection; his duty is to impeach and dare not avoid the power of the favored—thereby to keep clear what eyes and ears should see.
translations.idiomatic: He said a censor must not flinch from favorites.

sentences.zh: 按尚書令昌奏僕射崇，下獄復治，榜掠將死，卒無一辭，道路稱冤。

translations.literal: Pursuant to Master of Writing Zhao Chang's memorial on Vice Director Chong, Chong was sent back to prison for retrial, was flogged near death, yet to the end spoke not one word of confession—the roads cried injustice.
translations.idiomatic: He described Zheng Chong's silent endurance under the rod.

sentences.zh: 疑昌與崇內有纖介，浸潤相陷，自禁門內樞機近臣，蒙受冤譖，虧損國家，為謗不小。

translations.literal: I suspect Chang and Chong harbor a hair's-breadth grudge and drip slander to trap each other—yet from the inner gate, pivoting ministers close to the throne, suffering false accusation damages the state and slander is no small matter.
translations.idiomatic: He accused Zhao Chang of dripping malice against a palace officer.

sentences.zh: 臣請治昌，以解眾心。」

translations.literal: Your subject begs to try Chang to settle the multitude's hearts."
translations.idiomatic: He asked to put Zhao Chang on trial."

sentences.zh: 書奏，天子不說，以寶名臣不忍誅，乃制詔丞相、大司空：「司隸寶奏故尚書僕射崇冤，請獄治尚書令昌。

translations.literal: When the memorial was submitted the Son of Heaven was displeased; because Bao was a famed minister he could not bear to execute him and therefore ordered the chancellor and grand minister of works by edict: "Metropolitan Governor Bao memorializes that former Vice Director of the Masters of Writing Chong was wronged and begs to send Master of Writing Zhao Chang to prison for trial.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor told the chancellor that Sun Bao had demanded Zhao Chang's arrest for framing Zheng Chong.

sentences.zh: 案崇近臣，罪惡暴著，而寶懷邪，附下罔上，以春月作詆欺，遂其奸心，蓋國之賊也。

translations.literal: Pursuant to Chong, a close minister, his crimes and wickedness were glaringly manifest, yet Bao harbors wickedness, curries favor below and deceives above, in spring month practices slander, following his treacherous heart—he is truly a thief of the state.
translations.idiomatic: The rescript called Sun Bao a state-thief for springtime slander.

sentences.zh: 傳不雲乎？

translations.literal: Does the tradition not say?
translations.idiomatic: The tradition asks whether glib speakers do not ruin states.

sentences.zh: 『惡利口之覆國家。』

translations.literal: 'I hate the sharp tongue that overturns states.'
translations.idiomatic: The line condemns the clever mouth that pulls a dynasty down.

sentences.zh: 其免寶為庶人。」

translations.literal: Let him be dismissed as a commoner."
translations.idiomatic: Strip Sun Bao to commoner rank."

sentences.zh: 哀帝崩，王莽白王太后徵寶以為光祿大夫，與王舜等俱迎中山王。

translations.literal: When Emperor Ai died, Wang Mang reported to Queen Mother Wang and summoned Bao as grandee of the palace to welcome the King of Zhongshan together with Wang Shun and others.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Mang recalled him to greet the boy emperor.

sentences.zh: 平帝立，寶為大司農。

translations.literal: When Emperor Ping was established, Bao became grand minister of agriculture.
translations.idiomatic: Under Pingdi he ran the granaries as grand minister of agriculture.

sentences.zh: 會越巂郡上黃龍游江中，太師孔光、大司徒馬宮等咸稱莽功德比周公，宜告祠宗廟。

translations.literal: It happened that Yuegui commandery reported a yellow dragon swimming in the river; Grand Tutor Kong Guang, grand minister of works Ma Gong, and others all praised Wang Mang's merit and virtue as comparable to the Duke of Zhou and said it should be announced at the ancestral temple.
translations.idiomatic: A yellow-dragon omen set ministers praising Wang Mang as another Duke of Zhou.

sentences.zh: 寶曰：「周公上聖，召公大賢，尚猶有不相說，著於經典，兩不相損。

translations.literal: Bao said, "The Duke of Zhou was a supreme sage, the Duke of Shao a great worthy, yet still they did not fully agree, as the classics record, and neither side was harmed.
translations.idiomatic: Sun Bao said Zhou and Shao had quarreled without ruining the state.

sentences.zh: 今風雨未時，百姓不足，每有一事，群臣同聲，得無非其美者。」

translations.literal: Now wind and rain are not timely, the hundred surnames are not sufficient; at every affair the ministers speak with one voice—may there not be something not beautiful in it?"
translations.idiomatic: He asked whether unanimous praise while the people starved was truly a good sign."

sentences.zh: 時，大臣皆失色，侍中奉車都尉甄邯即時承製罷議者。

translations.literal: At the time the great ministers all changed color; attendant and bearer of the chariot Zhen Han immediately received the edict pattern and dismissed those discussing the matter.
translations.idiomatic: The court froze; Zhen Han cut off debate under edict.

sentences.zh: 會寶遣吏迎母，母道病，留弟家，獨遣妻子。

translations.literal: It happened that Bao sent a clerk to welcome his mother; on the road she fell ill and stayed at his younger brother's house, and he sent only wife and children.
translations.idiomatic: He sent men for his mother but she stayed ill at his brother's while only his wife and children traveled.

sentences.zh: 司直陳崇以奏寶，事下三公即訊。

translations.literal: Straightener Chen Chong memorialized against Bao; the matter went down to the three dukes for immediate questioning.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Chong impeached the arrangement; the three dukes held a hearing.

sentences.zh: 寶對曰：「年七十悖眊，恩衰共養，營妻子，如章。」

translations.literal: Bao replied, "At seventy one is muddled; kindness to support a mother fails, and one schemes for wife and children—it is as stated in the memorial."
translations.idiomatic: Sun Bao pleaded age and family duty as his excuse.

sentences.zh: 寶坐免，終於家。

translations.literal: Bao sat conviction and was dismissed; he ended his life at home.
translations.idiomatic: He took dismissal and died at home.

sentences.zh: 建武中，錄舊德臣，以寶孫伉為諸長。

translations.literal: In the Jianwu era, recording old virtuous ministers, the court appointed Bao's grandson Kang as a chief.
translations.idiomatic: Later Guangwudi honored the family with a post for his grandson Kang.

sentences.zh: 毌將隆

translations.literal: Wujiang Long.
translations.idiomatic: Wujiang Long.

sentences.zh: 毌將隆字君房，東海蘭陵人也。

translations.literal: Wujiang Long, courtesy name Junfang, was a man of Lanling in the Eastern Sea.
translations.idiomatic: Wujiang Long, styled Junfang, came from Lanling in the Eastern Sea.

sentences.zh: 大司馬車騎將軍王音內領尚書，外典兵馬，踵故選置從事中郎與參謀議，奏請隆為從事中郎，遷諫大夫。

translations.literal: Grand marshal and chariot-and-cavalry general Wang Yin held the masters of writing within and commanded troops without; following precedent he selected staff advisers for counsel and policy, memorialized requesting Long as staff adviser, and promoted him to grandee remonstrant.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Yin staffed his headquarters and pulled Long up to remonstrant.

sentences.zh: 成帝末，隆奏封事言：「古老選諸侯入為公卿，以褒功德，宜徵定陶王使在國邸，以填萬方。」

translations.literal: Near the end of Emperor Cheng's reign Long submitted a sealed memorial saying, "In high antiquity feudal lords were chosen to enter as dukes and ministers to reward merit and virtue; you should summon the King of Dingtao to lodge in the capital residence and thereby steady the four quarters."
translations.idiomatic: He urged Chengdi to bring the King of Dingtao to the capital as heir apparent material.

sentences.zh: 其後上竟立定陶王為太子，隆遷翼州牧、穎川太守。

translations.literal: Later the emperor did establish the King of Dingtao as heir apparent; Long was promoted to shepherd of Ji Province and governor of Yingchuan.
translations.idiomatic: Chengdi did adopt the Dingtao prince; Long won Ji Province and Yingchuan.

sentences.zh: 哀帝即位，以高第入為京兆尹，遷執金吾。

translations.literal: When Emperor Ai ascended, Long by top evaluation entered as metropolitan governor and was promoted to Bearer of the Mace.
translations.idiomatic: Ai made him metropolitan governor, then Bearer of the Mace.

sentences.zh: 時，侍中董賢方貴，上使中黃門發武庫兵，前後十輩，送董賢及上乳母王阿捨。

translations.literal: At the time Gentleman Attendant Dong Xian was newly exalted; the emperor had palace yellow gates issue arms from the arsenal, ten batches in succession, to deliver to Dong Xian and the emperor's wet nurse Wang A's house.
translations.idiomatic: Eunuchs hauled ten wagonloads of arsenal weapons to Dong Xian and the nurse Wang.

sentences.zh: 隆奏曰：「武庫兵器，天下公用，國家武備，繕治造作，皆度大司農錢。

translations.literal: Long memorialized: "Arsenal weapons are for public use under heaven, the state's military equipment; repair and manufacture are all measured against Grand Minister of Agriculture funds.
translations.idiomatic: Wujiang Long protested that ordnance belongs to the state treasury.

sentences.zh: 大司農錢自乘輿不以給共養，共養勞賜，一出少府。

translations.literal: Grand Minister of Agriculture funds are not taken from the imperial carriage for allowances; allowances and gifts for support all come from the privy treasury.
translations.idiomatic: The privy purse feeds the harem, not the granary budget.

sentences.zh: 蓋不以本臧給末用，不以民力共浮費，別公私，示正路也。

translations.literal: Thus the root storehouse is not spent on branch uses nor the people's strength wasted on floating expense—this separates public from private and shows the straight road.
translations.idiomatic: Keeping granary coin off petty spending was how Han drew the line between palace and state.

sentences.zh: 古者諸侯方伯得顓征伐，乃賜斧鉞，漢家邊吏，職在距寇，亦賜武庫兵，皆任其事然後蒙之。

translations.literal: In antiquity regional lords and border shepherds who might wage war alone were then given axes and halberds; Han border officers whose duty is to repel raiders may also be granted arsenal arms—only after they shoulder the task do they receive them.
translations.idiomatic: He traced how axes and arsenal issues were tied to real military duty.

sentences.zh: 《春秋》之誼，家不臧甲，所以抑臣威，損私力也。

translations.literal: By Spring and Autumn principle a household does not hoard armor—this checks ministerial might and cuts private force.
translations.idiomatic: The Spring and Autumn canon forbade private armor to clip ministerial muscle.

sentences.zh: 今賢等便僻弄臣，私恩微妾，而以天下公用給其私門，契國威器共其家備。

translations.literal: Now Xian and his like are fawning favorites and private-favor concubines, yet the empire's public stores supply their private gates, and the state's awesome weapons stock their household gear.
translations.idiomatic: He said giving state arms to Dong Xian and a nurse turned public gear into private toys.

sentences.zh: 民力分於弄臣，武兵設於微妾，建立非宜，以廣驕僭，非所以示四方也。

translations.literal: The people's strength is split among favorites, war arms are set out for petty concubines—establishments improper in this way widen arrogance and usurpation; it is not the way to show the four quarters.
translations.idiomatic: He warned that arming playthings would teach the realm contempt.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：『奚取於三家之堂！』

translations.literal: Confucius said, 'What can be taken from the hall of the three families!'
translations.idiomatic: He quoted Confucius on the three Huan arsenals.

sentences.zh: 臣請收還武庫。」

translations.literal: Your subject begs to collect the weapons back into the arsenal."
translations.idiomatic: He asked the throne to recall every blade."

sentences.zh: 上不說。

translations.literal: The emperor was displeased.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor sulked.

sentences.zh: 頃之，傅太后使謁者買諸官婢，賤取之，復取執金吾官婢八人。

translations.literal: Before long Empress Dowager Fu had her attendants buy palace slave-girls at low prices and also took eight slave-girls from the Bearer of the Mace.
translations.idiomatic: Grand Dowager Fu's buyers underpaid palace women and skimmed Bearer of the Mace maids.

sentences.zh: 隆奏言賈賤，請更平直。

translations.literal: Long memorialized that the purchases were too cheap and requested fair reassessment.
translations.idiomatic: Wujiang Long demanded fair prices for the sales.

sentences.zh: 上於是制詔丞相、御史大夫：「交讓之禮興，則虞、芮之訟息。

translations.literal: The emperor therefore ordered the chancellor and imperial counsellor by edict: "When the rite of yielding flourishes, the dispute of Yu and Rui ceases.
translations.idiomatic: The rescript opened with a lecture on yielding like Yu and Rui.

sentences.zh: 隆位九卿，既無以匡朝廷之不逮，而反奏請與永信宮爭貴賤之賈，程奏顯言，眾莫不聞。

translations.literal: Long stands among the Nine Ministers yet has not corrected what the court lacks; instead he memorializes to wrangle with the Yongxin Palace over the price of slave-girls, stating it openly in memorials that everyone has heard.
translations.idiomatic: It scolded him for haggling with the Fu palace over slave prices.

sentences.zh: 舉錯不由誼理，爭求之名自此始，無以示百僚，傷化失俗。」

translations.literal: His actions do not follow proper principle; the name of wrangling begins here—it cannot be shown to the hundred officials and wounds transformation and loses custom."
translations.idiomatic: The edict called the quarrel vulgar and demoralizing."

sentences.zh: 以隆前有安國之言，左遷為沛郡都尉，遷南郡太守。

translations.literal: Because Long earlier had sound words on state peace he was demoted to chief commandant of Pei commandery and transferred to governor of Nan Commandery.
translations.idiomatic: He was busted to Pei, then sent south, for having once spoken well of the state.

sentences.zh: 王莽少時，慕與隆交，隆不甚附。

translations.literal: When Wang Mang was young he admired making friends with Long; Long did not much attach himself.
translations.idiomatic: Young Wang Mang courted him; Long kept his distance.

sentences.zh: 哀帝崩，莽秉政，使大司徒孔光奏隆前為冀州牧治中山馮太后獄冤陷無辜，不宜處位在中土。

translations.literal: When Emperor Ai died, Wang Mang seized government and had Grand Minister of Works Kong Guang memorialize that Long, as former shepherd of Ji Province, had handled the Zhongshan Lady Feng case unjustly and trapped the innocent—he should not hold office in the central plain.
translations.idiomatic: Mang used the old Lady Feng inquest to exile Long from the heartland.

sentences.zh: 本中謁者令史立、侍御史丁玄自典考之，但與隆連名奏事。

translations.literal: Originally Director of Palace Attendants Shi Li and attendant clerk Ding Xuan personally conducted the examination; they only joined Long's name on the memorial.
translations.idiomatic: Shi Li and Ding Xuan had run the trial; Long had only cosigned.

sentences.zh: 史立時為中太僕，丁玄奏山太守，及尚書令趙昌譖鄭崇者為河內太守，皆免官，徙合浦。

translations.literal: Shi Li was then grand coachman of the palace, Ding Xuan was governor of Taishan, and Master of Writing Zhao Chang—who had slandered Zheng Chong—was governor of Henei; all were dismissed from office and relocated to Hepu.
translations.idiomatic: Mang cashiered Shi Li, Ding Xuan, and Zhao Chang to Hepu.

sentences.zh: 何併字子廉，祖父以吏二千石自平輿徙平陵。

translations.literal: He Bing, courtesy name Zilian, was a man whose grandfather moved from Pingyu to Pingling as a clerk at two thousand piculs.
translations.idiomatic: He Bing's grandfather relocated to Pingling on a clerk's salary.

sentences.zh: 併為郡吏，至大司空掾，事何武。

translations.literal: Bing was a commandery clerk and rose to assistant under the grand minister of works, serving He Wu.
translations.idiomatic: He rose to staff under Grand Minister of Works He Wu.

sentences.zh: 武高其志節，舉能治劇，為長陵令，道不拾遺。

translations.literal: Wu admired his integrity and recommended him as able to govern a severe district; he became magistrate of Changling, where things dropped on the road were not picked up.
translations.idiomatic: He Wu sent him to tough Changling, where honesty ran so high that lost goods stayed put.

sentences.zh: 初，邛成太后外家王氏貴，而侍中王林卿通輕俠，傾京師。

translations.literal: Earlier the maternal kin of Empress Dowager of Qiongcheng, the Wangs, were noble, while Gentleman Attendant Wang Linqing consorted with bravos and tilted the capital.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Linqing of the empress's clan ran with swordsmen and owned the city.

sentences.zh: 後坐法免，賓客愈盛，歸長陵上塚，因留飲連日。

translations.literal: Later he was dismissed for breaking the law, yet his retainers grew more numerous; returning to Changling to sweep a tomb he stayed drinking for days.
translations.idiomatic: Stripped of office, he threw bigger parties at the family tombs.

sentences.zh: 並恐其犯法，自造門上謁，謂林卿曰：「塚間單外，君宜以時歸。」

translations.literal: Bing feared he would break the law, personally visited his gate, and said to Linqing, "The tomb district is lonely and exposed—you should return in good time."
translations.idiomatic: He Bing warned Wang to leave the lonely graveyard before trouble came.

sentences.zh: 林卿曰：「諾。」

translations.literal: Linqing said, "Agreed."
translations.idiomatic: "Very well," said Wang Linqing.

sentences.zh: 先是，林卿殺婢婿埋塚捨，併具知之，以非己時，又見其新免。

translations.literal: Earlier Linqing had killed his maidservant's husband and buried him in the tomb keeper's hut; Bing knew the whole story but because it was not his watch and because Linqing had just been dismissed,
translations.idiomatic: He knew Wang had buried a body at the tomb but held his peace while Wang was newly disgraced.

sentences.zh: 故不發舉，欲無令留界中而已，即且遣吏奉謁傳送。

translations.literal: he did not expose it, wishing only that Linqing not linger in his jurisdiction, and therefore sent clerks with formal visiting cards to escort him onward.
